This isn't in the 0.4.0 spec.

Hopefully the development version of the spec will go stable soon, per toml-lang/toml#30

> (well at least similar to how the browser does joins)

Browsers don't join `http://domain.com:9999/` and `http://:9021/` into
`http://domain.com:9021/` either, though. For example, a quick test:

 * https://jsbin.com/lufefupota/edit?html,output

In both Chrome and Firefox, the `click me` anchor doesn't navigate to

 * https://jsbin.com:9021/lufefupota/edit?html,output

It doesn't navigate anywhere.


Do you have an example of a piece of software (application, library, tool, etc)
that joins `http://domain.com:9999/` and `http://:9021/` into
`http://domain.com:9021/`?

after using for more than 10 seconds, it appears this is already supported.  I suppose my question is "how", so that I can add the appropriate deps (if any) for other users.

The status code 1 happened because of the input parameters. I was manually setting `_cmd` and `cmd` values to a string for testing purposes. So, I made sure to send them as a list now. But, now the status code is returned as 2.

The exact same command runs absolutely fine when I run it on the command line. But, when I do it through python, it returns status code 2. The one difference I noticed is the way the recording is being done:

In python
```
frame=  119 fps= 29 q=28.0 size=       0kB time=00:00:02.40 bitrate=   0.2kbits/s speed=0.594x
```

In plain command line
```
frame=  294 fps= 26 q=-1.0 Lsize=     528kB time=00:00:11.64 bitrate= 371.7kbits/s speed=1.03x
```

With the same exact command running, the way the recording is happening is very different. It looks like the python version has very bad quality. Is it possible that this is happening because of running it inside a python thread? And perhaps, that's why it's exiting with a status code 2. Because as we can see above, bitrate is awful and so is the speed -- so basically nothing is getting recording.

Also, as an alternative that works, until this is fixed, you can do the following:

```python
mail_request = esiclient.request(op, raw_body_only=True)
import json
mails = json.loads(mails.raw)
```

So you'll have at least your data.

Also, I would recommend going to Settings in gdbgui and turning on the option `Print all sent commands in console, including those sent automatically by gdbgui`. This way, you can see exactly what is being sent by gdbgui. You can also run the same commands you had working in command line gdb, but manually in gdbgui and see if it works that way. 

In regard to your second comment, I am glad to hear it. I agree that existing solutions feel a little clunky, which is why I started the project in the first place, so I'm very pleased that it's working (at least somewhat) for you. And please keep filing issues! The best way to make gdbgui better is to let me know your use case and pain points so I can address them. FWIW, here is the best I have managed to achieve so far with the MQTT Template Light platform to control my IKEA Tradfri Lights (on/off and level control working):

```
light:
  - platform: mqtt_template
    command_topic: "zigate/command"
    command_on_template: >
      {%- if brightness is defined -%}
      {"function": "action_move_level_onoff", "args": ["5c2f", 1, 1, {{ brightness / 255 * 100 }}]}
      {%- else -%}
      {"function": "action_onoff", "args": ["5c2f", 1, 1]}
      {%- endif -%}
    command_off_template: '{"function": "action_onoff", "args": ["5c2f", 1, 0]}'
    optimistic: true
    brightness_template: '' # Not None to allow controlling brightness
    state_topic: "zigate/attribute_changed/5c2f/01/0006/0000"
    state_template: '{{ value_json["value"] }}'
```

This still misses light level state changes and initial device state messages pushed to `zigate/device_changed/5c2f`.

Now, none of the current MQTT light platforms is gathering all required features to allow for a perfect fit:

* `light.mqtt` is missing command templates features to be able to format command messages as JSON
* `light.mqtt_template` is missing a `brightness_state_topic` option to process `zigate/attribute_changed/5c2f/01/0008/0000` messages.
* `light.mqtt_json` is missing command templates and a `brightness_state_topic` option to process `zigate/attribute_changed/5c2f/01/0008/0000` messages.

At this point, I'm considering to adapt your project (what's the license by the way?) to send all device and attribute change events to the same topic and use the `mqtt.light_template` platform some configuration like the following:

```
light:
  - platform: mqtt_template
    command_topic: "zigate/command"
    command_on_template: >
      {%- if brightness is defined -%}
      {"function": "action_move_level_onoff", "args": ["5c2f", 1, 1, {{ brightness / 255 * 100 }}]}
      {%- else -%}
      {"function": "action_onoff", "args": ["5c2f", 1, 1]}
      {%- endif -%}
    command_off_template: '{"function": "action_onoff", "args": ["5c2f", 1, 0]}'
    state_topic: "zigate/device_changed/5c2f"
    state_template: '{{ value_json["endpoints"][0]["clusters"][0]["attributes"]["value"] }}'

```

- wait for a new release of requests-toolbelt and upgrade to it
- for other, similar situations (where the default adapter class isn't user): as @sigmavirus24 suggested, for zenpy to provide an `adapter_class` parameter in the client `__init__` method which defaults to `requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter` but can be customized. (and then, when re-mounting the transport do something like `session.mount('https://', self.adapter_class(max_retries=3))`)

Hi @kblakshmi! By default `zap-cli` will only output "High" alerts that are found (i.e. XSS, CSRF, SQLi, etc.). If you also want to output lower level alerts like the X-Frame-Options header being missing, you can use the `--alert-level` or `-l` parameter with the `quick-scan` command (which can be either High, Medium, Low, Informational).

So, if you want to output all alerts, you could use "Informational" with your command:
```
zap-cli -p 8080 --zap-path /home/ubuntu/ZAP_2.6.0 quick-scan -l Informational -s xss,sqli --spider -r http://www.example.com
```

You can also change that to Low or Medium to output fewer alerts by only including those of a higher severity.

For the other questions:

> a. what other options can be set as argument as -s xss, sqli and all.

The groups you can use are all, sqli, xss_persistent, xss_reflected, and xss, which are just aliases around groups of scan IDs. You can also enable individual scanners by their ID. You can see the list of available scanners and their IDs by running `zap-cli scanners list`. So, for example, if you wanted to enable all XSS scanners as well as "External Redirect" (20019) and "Buffer Overflow" (30001), you could run:
```
zap-cli -p 8080 --zap-path /home/ubuntu/ZAP_2.6.0 quick-scan -l Informational -s xss,20019,30001 --spider -r http://www.example.com
```

> b. can authentication be performed using zap-cli (our application has login screen - oauth implemention)

Not right now. This is currently on my todo list to add support for the authentication methods in ZAP and scanning as a user for a given context, so hopefully will be available soon. Currently in my CI pipeline, as a workaround, I use `zap-cli` to control scanning as different users by having `httpsender` scripts that set the appropriate `access_token` for the user for all the requests to the site, with the access_token being retrieved from the OAuth server in advance of running the scan. Then I use `zap-cli scripts` to load, enable, and disable the scripts before each scan run. But, I hope to add proper support for the authentication methods in ZAP soon.

I hope that helps!

The question is to solve the issue on WINDOWS!!

tl;dr; Try 

```
snmpwalk.py   -v 3 -u user1 -l noAuthNoPriv 172.16.2.94 sysName
```

The thing is that `snmpwalk` first tries to query the exact object you give it (with SNMP GET command), then goes on with the GETNEXT iterations.

The `snmpwalk.py` tool does not do `GET` so it goes straight to GETNEXT which probably returns the "next" object which immediately goes out of scope so the output is empty.

Consider:

```
$ snmpwalk.py -v2c -c public demo.snmplabs.com SNMPv2-MIB::sysDescr.0
$ snmpwalk.py -v2c -c public demo.snmplabs.com SNMPv2-MIB::sysDescr
SNMPv2-MIB::sysDescr.0 = DisplayString: Linux zeus 4.8.6.5-smp #2 SMP Sun Nov 13 14:58:11 CDT 2016 i686 
```

The original motivation for *gmaps* was to help explore geographical data. For this use case, most users will have data in arrays that are built from databases or CSV files, as @reyale said. However, I'm not convinced this makes an interface like `Heatmap(lat=[...], lng=[...])` necessarily the right choice. After all, something like:

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv(...)
Heatmap(data=df[["latitude", "longitude"]])
```

... is just as obvious. Clearly latitude and longitude belong together and passing in one without the other doesn't make much sense. Note that this doesn't work at the moment, but fixing it is easy and doesn't change the interface (see issue #66).

I can see the case for having split `lat=`, `lng=` keyword arguments but I don't think the added benefit (if any) really justify the breaking API changes etc, as well as the extra development work that this would entail. I'd much rather concentrate on adding new layers and adding the ability to customise existing layers than on changing the API.

here's a server that sends data with gzip+chunked transfer encoding:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import socket
import time
import zlib

def main():
	s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
	s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
	s.bind(('', 8000))
	s.listen(4)
	while True:
		conn, addr = s.accept()
		handle(conn)

def handle(conn):
	print('<-\n' + conn.recv(4096).decode('utf-8'))
	headers = [
		'HTTP/1.1 200 OK',
		'Content-Type: text/plain',
		'Transfer-Encoding: gzip, chunked',
	]
	send(conn, ('\r\n'.join(headers) + '\r\n\r\n').encode())
	time.sleep(0.1)

	compress = zlib.compressobj(level=0, wbits=9)
	for i in range(4):
		chunk = compress.compress(b'a' * 100)
		if len(chunk) > 0:
			send_chunk(conn, chunk)
			time.sleep(0.1)
	send_chunk(conn, compress.flush())
	send_chunk(conn, b'')
	conn.close()

def send(conn, data):
	print('->\n', data)
	conn.send(data)

def send_chunk(conn, chunk):
	send(conn, hex(len(chunk))[2:].encode() + b'\r\n' + chunk + b'\r\n')

if __name__ == '__main__':
	main()
```
which works if you `curl localhost:8000`

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import socket
import zlib

def main():
	conn = socket.create_connection(('localhost', 8000))
	conn.send(b'meh')
	headers = conn.recv(1024) # I sure hope this is only the headers
	decompress = zlib.decompressobj()
	while True:
		raw = conn.recv(1024)
		if not raw:
			break
		print('<-', raw)

		hex_length, chunk, other = raw.split(b'\r\n', 2)
		data = decompress.decompress(chunk)
		print('decompressed:', data)
	data = decompress.flush()
	print('decompressed:', data)

if __name__ == '__main__':
	main()
```

Can we take advantages from new **vim 8.0** packages..?
**_Asynchronous I/O support, channels ~_**

Vim can now exchange messages with other processes in the background.  This
makes it possible to have servers do work and send back the results to Vim.
See |channel-demo| for an example, this shows communicating with a Python
server.

Closely related to channels is JSON support.  JSON is widely supported and can
easily be used for inter-process communication, allowing for writing a server
in any language.  The functions to use are |json_encode()| and |json_decode()|.

This makes it possible to build very complex plugins, written in any language
and running in a separate process.


**_Jobs ~_**

Vim can now start a job, communicate with it and stop it.  This is very useful
to run a process for completion, syntax checking, etc.  Channels are used to
communicate with the job.  Jobs can also read from or write to a buffer or a
file.  See |job_start()|.


_**Timers ~**_

Also asynchronous are timers.  They can fire once or repeatedly and invoke a
function to do any work.  For example: >
	let tempTimer = timer_start(4000, 'CheckTemp')
This will call the CheckTemp() function four seconds (4000 milli seconds)
later.  See |timer_start()|.

Hello,

You could implement your changes without modifying the library code by just extending `OpenAPISchemaGenerator`:

```python
class CustomSchemaGenerator(OpenAPISchemaGenerator):
    def get_schema(self, request=None, public=False):
        swagger = super(CustomSchemaGenerator, self).get_schema(request, public)
        swagger.consumes = ['application/vnd.api+json']
        swagger.produces = ['application/vnd.api+json']
        
        return swagger
```

And then you can pass it to [get_schema_view](https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/drf_yasg.html#drf_yasg.views.get_schema_view) as the `generator_class` argument.

Note however that `consumes` is also set at the Operation level, and you might have to change it there too - see [here](https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/custom_spec.html#swaggerautoschema), [here](https://drf-yasg.readthedocs.io/en/stable/drf_yasg.html#drf_yasg.generators.OpenAPISchemaGenerator.get_operation).

I've been thinking about this and the implementation that makes the most sense to me is a stack. Here's my proposal:

1. Load the file into a list (stack)
2. Add every link to that list during the session (don't even check for membership, if you visit an element again, it goes back on top of the stack)
3. On exit, pop the last N elements off of the list (using `stack.pop()`)
4. Write those links to the file.

Pros:
 - Order is maintained
 - Fewer file operations. One on launch and one on exit

Cons:
 - May be duplicate elements
 - Will not save history if they close the terminal

Transport/protocol separation is following:

* Protocol is responsible for a message format, e.g. it defines JSON object with `method` and `args`. This is what `json-rpc` library does, it is not aware of how you transfer the message.
* Transport is a way to pass your message to the server and back. It could be either API call (with Django, Flask, Asyncio, etc.) or you could communicate via messaging queue (RabbitMQ, Kafka).

Looks to me like there is a bug with `.guess_position` on this [line](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/17f2f866302383adaef6c73b00483ddf64263e6c/nflgame/player.py#L158).

```python
        if self.player is not None:
            return self.player.position
```

Problem there is that `self.player` is *never* `None`.  So it never gets into trying to deduce the position from the stats.

I changed that line to 
```python
        if self.player.position:
            return self.player.position
```
Now `nflgame` will try to guess the position if one is not present.

> I don't like some of the ways the modernize tool handles things. Right now I'm working on my own dual version compatible branch.

Well, suit yourself, but what exactly is the problem? Wouldn't just additional commit on the top of my branch be enough? The authors of both ``modernize`` and ``six`` have a lot of experience with this sort of thing, and I (with my limited experience of porting one large and hugely complicated project to Py2/3k version) don't see much wrong with their decisions.

> One of the issues I've come across is differences in the versions of six.py running around. Calibre includes six 1.10.0. The version of six that installs for Py 3.7 with pip for me is the last released version of 1.11.0--which doesn't include everything in the latest git version, especially ensure_text(). I'm not sure how I want to address that yet.

Take a look at https://six.readthedocs.io/, particularly at the sentence at the top of the page: 

> six consists of only one Python file, so it is painless to copy into a project.

It took me a lot of time and plenty of broken packages in Fedora/RHEL to accept as a fact, that ``six`` is really best treated like that. Yes, we all Linux packagers believe in the constant unbundling (and it is The Right Thing™), but ``six`` is probably really the exception which confirms the rule.

The best thing in my opinion (and what I did in M2Crypto and here in this branch) is just take the latest version of ``six`` from github and stick it to your project.

Also, ``six.ensure_text`` is really lovely thing to have, and I had to have my own version of it in M2Crypto with the previous versions of ``six``, so it kind of makes sense to have the official blessed version.

As somebody that uses both Python-CAN and Micropython I feel it would be hard to get a unified API. Microcontrollers are just very architecture specific, so you see differences just in between ports due to this. Send and receive because is easier to abstract, but lower level controls and other management might be too limited or too complex depending on device.

My suggestion would be to make a uPython-CAN library that does not impact the much more optimized main library.  Microcontrollers are just so resourced constrained that I would not want to or try to get a desktop application to act like or mimic that world. Micropython already is forced to make compromises calls for efficiency and resources. 

It might also be good to clarify the goal of this, because it is bridging two worlds and could easily be a lot of work, Micropython being for bare metal, and Python being for full desktops.

Yeah, I know about pytest fixtures, but I basically don't think it's possible to use the combination of Hypothesis and them like this - you could use something like strategy(something).example() to generate the fixture (don't do that, it has problems for other reasons), but I'm almost 100% certain you can't get assume in a fixture to work.

Fundamentally the problem is that Hypothesis and pytest have a very different notion of how tests are executed. Hypothesis 

In general Hypothesis and pytest have very different ideas about how tests should be executed. Integration between the two of them basically happens by Hypothesis going to some length to stay out of pytest's way and expose an interface it can use - Using given for some arguments will not prevent fixtures, parametrize, etc. from work with another, but that's about the best I can do.

The major limitations you'd run into are a) Hypothesis mixes example generation and test execution, which the py.test test generator really doesn't like and b) Once Hypothesis has found a failure, it has to minimize it.

b is also the major problem for treating Hypothesis as a fixture source. The examples Hypothesis generates are really messy and only become readable after the minimization process. If Hypothesis were just providing examples and not minimizing, which is what the fixture mode of operation requires, it would be basically useless.

I'm also not really clear why you want this. Are you just worried about the performance implications of running the generation process multiple times? If so, is this actually proving a problem for you or is it just a theoretical concern? If the former maybe you're hitting a Hypothesis bug because generation is usually pretty fast.

Also, for the tests you linked to, have you seen [the support for mapping strategies](http://hypothesis.readthedocs.org/en/latest/data.html#mapping)? You might find it useful given your data conversion at the beginning of each test.

Could the plugin not simply run the preview through the same Markdown processor as the main library? I guess you still have the potential problem of an open markdown tag, but that's less problematic than open HTML tags.

The preferred way would be to include intro snippets as metadata. You can embed metadata in the markdown post as shown here:
https://pythonhosted.org/Markdown/extensions/meta_data.html

For instance, you can write a intro as:

    Intro: This is a summary for the post.
    This is the long post that goes on and on.    

Now the metadata is passed down to the view in the variable `meta`. You can use this variable in your modified `index.html` using `meta.intro`.

We don't need to modify and make new release to accomadate this feature.

@sebdiem The changes makes ```example.py``` fail to run due to the ```iterable``` kwargs taking the replacing the positional arg ```arcname```.

I think it would be a more suitable solution to create a ```zipstream.ZipFile.write_iter(arcname, iter[, compress_type])``` method instead of modify ```write```.
* Keeps the function signature the same as stdlib's ```zipfile.ZipFile.write(filename[, arcname[, compress_type]])```
* ```arcname``` would be a required positional argument instead of kwarg.
* Let's ```example.py``` work again
* There is a similar method/reference ```zipfile.ZipFile.writestr(zinfo_or_arcname, bytes[, compress_type])```

> I don't like "Magic Delay Fairies"

Issuing sleeps when using eventlet/gevent is not for delay purposes. A `sleep(0)` call is the mechanism by which the current task releases the CPU to give it to another waiting tasks. This is the foundation on which multi-tasking for green threads works. It's not a delay, you are passing 0, so it is not going to make anything slower.

But make sure when you call `time.sleep(0)` that you have monkey patched the Python standard library at the start of your application, because eventlet has a special version of `sleep()` that needs to be used. Without monkey patching you are probably going to have trouble, things are going to get stuck.

There's a few settings (and design choices) that could affect how many words the generator loads from a wordlist. 

The list gets deduplicated, so if you created your list by joining other lists this could be a factor.

The default command line options load in words between 5 and 9 characters long. Both bounds are fairly arbitrary, and were picked to enforce minimum total password length and to stop users getting esoteric technical or scientific words in passwords (this is a severe issue when using the Linux standard dict wordlists). You could run `xkcdpass -V --min 1 --max 100` or similar to see how significant this effect is.

If you test those and are still missing words let me know. The only other thing I can think of is that the regex filtering for `--valid-chars` is causing problems but that doesn't seem likely.

All countries for which a VAT number regex is available, are accepted by default. I don't feel that accepting just anything for unknown countries is a good solution. If you want to suggest new regexes, feel free to add them to this issue or submit a pull request.

I have modified the code to not imply `eu_only` anymore when `vies_check` is set. If the latter is `True`, only VAT numbers from the EU VAT area are validated against VIES, other VAT numbers remain unaffected.

However, please note that the reason for implying `eu_only` was a regulatory one: if you must validate against VIES, you must also use `eu_only`. I have included a note for this in the documentation.

These fixes will be included in a future release.

The code to the myCSVIter is now as follows:

```
class myCSVIter(mx.io.DataIter):
    """ This class builds a custom CSV iterator that returns the data in the same format
    a NDArrayIter would, when provided two column vectors.

    The code has been built by looking into the NDArrayIter implementation, and builds 
    data batches by iterating with csv.reader over the file. When the iterator is reset,
    the pointer of the file is set to 0 and a new csv.reader iterator is created.

    :param data_names: The data names to be returned.
    :param data_shapes: The data shapes.
    :param csvfile: The CSV File to read from. Must have three columns (user,item,score).
    :param label_shapes: The label shapes.
    :param delimiter: The delimiter in the CSV file. Defaults to ','.
    :param batch_size: The size of the batches to read. Defaults to 100.
    """
    def __init__(self, data_names, data_shapes, label_names, csvfile,
                 label_shapes=(1,), delimiter=',', batch_size=100, context=mx.cpu(0)):
        from mxnet.io import DataDesc
        from mxnet.ndarray import array
        from collections import OrderedDict
        import csv
        self.delimiter=delimiter
        self.batch_size = batch_size
        self.context = context
        self._provide_data = [
            DataDesc('user', (batch_size,), int),
            DataDesc('item', (batch_size,), int)
        ]
        self._provide_label = [DataDesc('softmax_label',(batch_size,),numpy.float32)]
        self.file = open(csvfile,'r')
        self.csvreader = csv.reader(self.file, delimiter=delimiter)
        # Initiate indices to as many empty sets as data_shapes says
        self.indices = [set() for i in range(0,data_shapes[0][0]+1)] # +1 for the label
        for l in self.csvreader:
            for i,c in enumerate([int(x) for x in l]):
                self.indices[i].add(c)
        self.maximum_values = [max(x) for x in self.indices]
        # Ensure that the CSV file is properly to the start for future iteration
        self.file.seek(0)


    def __iter__(self):
        return self

    def reset(self):
        import csv
        self.file.seek(0)
        # TODO: Does this leak the previous csvreader?
        self.csvreader = csv.reader(self.file, delimiter=self.delimiter)

    def __next__(self):
        return self.next()

    @property
    def provide_data(self):
        return self._provide_data

    @property
    def provide_label(self):
        return self._provide_label

    def next(self):
        l=[]; c=[]; v=[]
        try:
            # Build a batch by reading batch_size lines from the CSV file
            for i in range(self.batch_size):
                row = [int(a) for a in next(self.csvreader)]
                l.append(row[0])
                c.append(row[1])
                v.append(row[2])
            # Now build the three different NDArrays required
            data = [mx.nd.array(l, self.context),
                    mx.nd.array(c, self.context)]
            label= list([mx.nd.array(v,self.context)])
            # Return the batch itself
            return mx.io.DataBatch(data=list(data), label=label)
        except StopIteration:
            # If there is some data in the lcv arrays, it's the first time that
            # we reach the end of file, and the data in the lcv arrays needs to
            # be returned. StopIteration will be raised next time the iterator
            # is called, when the lcv arrays are empty.
            data_read = len(l)
            if data_read > 0:
                # There is some data. Pad and return.
                pad = self.batch_size - data_read
                for i in range(pad):
                  l.append(0)
                  c.append(0)
                  v.append(0)
                data = [mx.nd.array(l, self.context),
                        mx.nd.array(c, self.context)]
                label = list([mx.nd.array(v, self.context)])
                return mx.io.DataBatch(data=list(data), label=label, pad=pad)
            else:
                # No data. StopIteration can be raised now.
                raise StopIteration
```

`socket.recv()` will wait for a message to be available. If it's not returning, the odds are that there is no message waiting. You can use `socket.poll()` to check/wait for messages, or `recv(flags=zmq.NOBLOCK)` to raise `zmq.Again` if no message is available. If there's a problem, it's likely that a message you are expecting to send isn't sending, rather than a problem on the receiving side.

Looking at this, there are two related patches for the `Lazy/Cat.__add__` methods here.

One that checks for the presence of experimental.catalogqueryplan, and if it's present patches the lazy classes inside that package. That condition and patch can certainly go.

The other related patch applies the same change to the Lazy classes inside of Products.ZCatalog. That is ok for Zope 2.13/Products.ZCatalog 2.x/3.x. In more recent versions of those the Lazy module has moved and is now inside `ZTUtils.Lazy` (inside the Zope project).

I think the easiest way to get rid of all of these patches, would be to change the SolrResponse class in collective.solr and let it be a subsclass of Lazy. That way the `__add__` wouldn't have to be patched, as the `isinstance` check would succeed.

Thanks! Yes, I've been looking into `datetime.fromisoformat` intensely.

In cPython, it is actually implemented [both in Python](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/08c1da71030e0d6019b3493e0ffaf060a6aa5d8e/Lib/datetime.py#L1628) and [in C](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/08c1da71030e0d6019b3493e0ffaf060a6aa5d8e/Modules/_datetimemodule.c#L4828) and the C version is faster than any other method (including `ciso8601`).
I've been working on a [backport of it to older Python versions](https://github.com/movermeyer/backports.datetime_fromisoformat/). 

There [is an open issue](https://github.com/closeio/ciso8601/issues/64) to rewrite the docs to be more comprehensive and inclusive of alternatives.

This is nice, although it is not immediately clear to me how to make use of the resulting error structures. I'm not sure what use case you have designed for -- and I don't remember exactly what I was trying to do before myself, but I think it was for creating HTML forms with arbitrarily nested structures -- something most form validation libraries don't handle well.

Although, even from there, it does not seem straightforward to associate an error with a field -- it looks like I would have to reconstruct a key from the reverse of the context. An error structure that is made of a dictionary that reflects the original schema seems like it would be more straightforward to use. As for my part, I would probably want to dig a bit deeper on this before I said with certainty that a dictionary approach would be more useful.

In general, this seems good. Even for something like a JSON web API, which I understand to be Valideer's primary use case, it seems useful to be able to present all the errors, rather than shortcutting the validation.

But actually maybe we can potentially improve things. The rate count is supposedly incremented for every request to `/tracks/<id>/stream` and it seems we do at least *two* of those for every call to `translate_uri`. We do one in order to determine if we are allowed to stream the track and then another to get the URL when we actually come to play it. Seems to me we could do both those things at the same time and reduce this down to a single request. Technically I don't think you are meant to cache that redirect but we could try and see.

Potentially the Gstreamer internals might request it a few more times whilst playing, I'm not sure if it caches redirects or not. But if not, maybe we'll get even more of an improvement from this.

@woshihaoren I think what could work here is if you would import git after setting the environment variables, or by setting the environment variables from the shell invoking the python interpreter. Reason for that is that the environment variable is only read once at the time of import, and then cached in a [similarly named class variable](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/31ad0a0e2588819e791f4269a5d7d7e81a67f8cc/git/cmd.py#L247) in the `Git` type.

As you noted, there is currently no way to specify these arguments within the call. As a workaround, using environment variables seems to be working. Even though it appears slightly awkward as part of an API, I believe GitPython attempts to support standard git environment variables wherever possible.

To me it seems like a very viable amendment to support these parameters as part of the method signature. These could be added without breaking the API, and thus I would be very happy if you would consider to make a contribution. The difficulty of this task also makes it a good learning experience.

No problem, there's a lot of machinery working in the background of both pip and pipenv.

If you look at the original log you provided, you'll see the line:
`Using cached https://www.piwheels.hostedpi.com/simple/python-logstash/python_logstash-0.4.6-py3-none-any.whl`

This one of the official pypi servers, so perhaps Raspberry Pi is adding this with their python build? Either way, there's a few ways to get around this.

The first is to try clearing the cache with `pipenv lock --clear`. If that doesn't work then pip seems to be insisting on using the third party server as the primary. I'm not sure why they'd choose to do that but it's probably we shouldn't touch further than trying to clear.

If the above doesn't work, you can either use `pipenv run pip install -i https://pypi.python.org/simple python-logstash` to install from the primary pypi repo, or a less attractive method of `pipenv install --skip-lock` which will bypass the locking portion. This will be somewhat slower than using the lockfile but will bypass the current error when fetching from a 3rd party repo.

This kind of issue (markers in `install_requires`' requirements being ignored) looks like something that was fixed in [36.2.0](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/CHANGES.rst#v3620). Note that for compatibility with older versions, using `extras_require` is better:
```diff
  setup.py | 5 +++--
 1 file changed, 3 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git i/setup.py w/setup.py
index f1ef0b6..1e2ae50 100644
--- i/setup.py
+++ w/setup.py
@@ -40,8 +40,6 @@ test_deps = [
 
 install_deps = [
     'luma.core>=1.1.1',
-    'rpi_ws281x;platform_machine=="armv7l" and platform_system=="Linux"',
-    'ws2812;platform_machine=="armv7l" and platform_system=="Linux"'
 ]
 
 setup(
@@ -61,6 +59,9 @@ setup(
     setup_requires=pytest_runner,
     tests_require=["mock", "pytest", "pytest-cov"],
     extras_require={
+        ':platform_machine=="armv7l" and platform_system=="Linux"': [
+            'ws2812', 'rpi_ws281x',
+        ],
         'docs': [
             'sphinx >= 1.5.1'
         ],
```

I suspect this is because the script is signed so people don't need to change the execution policy to unrestricted. Placing the user-provided prompt inside the script will break the signature.

I wonder why sign the script at all. How many developers don't change the execution policy to Unrestricted anyway?

> `c:\Python36\python.exe -m venv venv`

You're using the stdlib venv, not virtualenv. To report this you should go to https://bugs.python.org. But the version installed when you set up a new virtual environment will always potentially be out of date - running `python -m pip install -U setuptools pip` should be pretty much the first thing you do in any new virtual environment.

A(nother) simple demonstration of the problem, which Homebrew rediscovered in https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/pull/8129:

```
$ virtualenv -p python3 test
$ test/bin/python3 -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'
/Users/tim/test/bin/python3

Backport of set literals, dict/set comprehension and `OrderedDict` in Python 2.7 makes it actually quite different from Python 2.6. Moreover, there have been numerous uses of these features in NetworkX 1.10 (in other words, NetworkX no longer "works perfectly fine on 2.6"). In fact, ordered graph classes, based on `OrderedDict`, is a major new feature in the 1.10 release. So there *is* good reason in dropping support for Python 2.6. Continuing to support Python 2.6 either prevents adoption of modern language features or creates extra development and maintenance burden. In particular, it is unreasonable to expect support for Python 2.6 till 2020, when Python 2.7 itself will also EOL after an exceptionally long lifetime. I believe that the more practical solution is to modify the setup script of SciPy and friends to use NetworkX 1.9.1 when testing against Python 2.6.

I agree, that would be a nice thing to have, although I've no idea how to approach it.
Basically speaking, we would like to have a fair web-deployed copy of documentation tree for each existing PR. That's a huge overhead.

Reasonable alternative is to enable `artifacts creation` for Travis (quick googling has indicated that there is such an option https://blog.travis-ci.com/2012-12-18-travis-artifacts/. In any case, at least AppVeyor has it), where on each commit a PR is not just being built, but also the outcomes of the building process are stored. This way, one could download automatically built copy of `skimage` and evaluate corresponding `.html` doc files. That is already much faster than manual building. To reduce the overhead for storing artifacts (I believe there is a limit of available storage for free accounts), we could save only `doc` folders as CI artifacts.
The most complex thing from my point of view is how do we select PRs of interest. At very rough estimate, only 20% of recent PRs introduce some changes to the documentation tree. Maybe we could use some of neat GitHub bots (for example, see https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/pull/3039#issuecomment-242317255 and https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/pull/3960#issuecomment-241488915) to detect if a PR changes anything in `doc` folder and trigger the corresponding option for Travis. This will, obviously, require some work to be implemented.

Finally, we have to developed a process to remove old/stored artifacts (applicable both for AppVeyor, Travis or even our website). Currently, I have no ideas how to make this work.

So there are pros and cons for the `main-2.0`.

Pro:
 - It's a security patch.
 - None of our parts uses the other possible serialization methods.

Con:
 - It's a breaking change in case someone uses external software with our broker.

The decision part is the following: Is the broker an API (documented or undocumented)? How high is the probability that someone uses it with another serialization method? And finally: is this amount high enough to satisfy that we postpone a security patch?

My own opinion is that admins or devs who are using our broker with external software (and other serialization methods) will understand the clearly marked change and can just set the right config flag. For installations which use the standard way, this is clearly a standard security update.

Pyunit always says "FAILED" in the end as long as it's not pass. But if you see the detailed output, it singles out errors. For our purposes, if something threw in `teardown_test`, then something went unexpectedly wrong, which translates to ERROR. Otherwise, a failed test with or without teardown error would be reported the same, and somebody would have to click the summary to read the extra errors to discover that something went unexpectedly wrong. That behavior would make teardown errors hard to discover, which is not good. Hence we intentionally change the test result to error. I don't really know what 3 means. if the test body explicitly Failed, then we won't move extra error to main. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/284)*

Are you comparing the `moments_sigma` outputs for the two cases?  If so, then I think what you're finding is what we expect.  The `moments_sigma` output is based on the determinant so it should be relatively insensitive to shear.  The issue we were having with the EstimateShear resolution factor is because it is based on the trace (which is what we want for noisy images because it's linear in the moments, but it has this problematic shape dependence).  

So the idea (I thought) was that you'd have the new code return the `moments_sigma` and `observed_shape` from the circularly-weighted moments, and we'd use that to get a trace estimate that is less sensitive to the shear.  But the determinant which is in `moments_sigma` is already insensitive to shape for a Gaussian model.

>  _newOrigin is supposed to return a new WCS. 

Yes.  Just about everything* in GalSim is designed to be immutable, so methods that change something return a new object with the applied change, leaving the original as it was.

*I believe the only exceptions are Image, since modifying images is kind of the point of GalSim, and the various random deviates whose internal states change every time they get used.

My guess is:

- Speed: maybe checking all the gradient norms and updating only the nonzero ones sequentially is not that much faster than just updating the full lookup parameter matrix (esp. if doing minibatch training in which case there might be a lot of non-zero gradients, and on GPU where full update is faster).

- Correctness: for optimizers with momentum like MomentumSGD and Adam you need to update parameters with zero gradient if they have non-zero momentum (whereas sparse update doesn't update parameters with zero gradient regardless).
This is done by e.g.: https://github.com/clab/dynet/commit/4127a363821f52ad095317bb14bd416e0bd93b21

Hi @thesamet. `PYSPARK_SUBMIT_ARGS` is currently completely managed by sparkly session and overwritten in order to set up access to the right repositories, packages, etc: https://github.com/Tubular/sparkly/blob/master/sparkly/session.py#L87. As a result, a user can't define anything through it as its value will be ignored.

Now, you could argue that maybe we should check its value first and try to merge whatever the user has already defined with whatever a sparkly session needs, but I think this would require more complicated logic to catch all the edge cases / conflicts, and also the scope of that env var is much wider than just setting `options` (in the sense of `SparklySession.options`), so I  think the solution proposed here is easier to maintain and use.

> Or use the `0.6.0` pex file to downgrade to 0.5? Or something else?

If you use 0.6.0 and run `dbrestore --latest`, then it will not detect and apply a 0.5.x backup. That would have to be an instruction like `dbrestore ~/.kolibri/backups/db-v0.5.0-??.dump`.

However, if the intention was to have a backup in hand in case 0.6.0 is bricked, then I don't think the restore plans should be depending on it.

.... so I think:

> Do you mean I should open up a python terminal and run similar commands?

...is actually the best way to single out 1 instruction (or script to download) for 0.5.x users. Either that, or we backport this whole thing, release it for 0.5.1 and don't tell anyone that they can restore on a version that's older.

> I think that concern is a little bit of a strawman. If a plugin is deactivated, of course the corresponding menu items should be hidden, no?

How? If this is done at build time (as you are suggesting) then it will not be deactivated when the plugin is deactivated. Precisely because it should be hidden if deactivated is why I have implemented a non-build time configuration based system.

> Maybe a stronger way to frame the critique is: my suggestion would probably make it harder to dynamically install and uninstall plugins that weren't originally present at build time.

If the information is there to deactivate a side nav item at run time, almost all systems I can conceive of for achieving this goal allow for both 'build time present' and 'build time absent' plugins to be activated and deactivated.

There is one scenario where I can imagine this not being the case: In order to achieve the ability to deactivate only build time plugins, at build time, metaprogramming is used to generate code to inject a 'activation' signal into the frontend showing that the items associated with this should be activated. What this is doing is basically doing what I am doing here with more magic (having a way of injecting which nav items should be activated through some plugin registry driven means), but has the drawback of disallowing non-build time present plugins from achieving the same thing.

> (I know we've talked in the past about how necessary - or possible - this is. Maybe it's gotten closer with the build decoupling system?)

The present architecture is still designed for the future goal of allowing this to happen, I think there is still an open question about *whether* this is something that people truly want (although, it certainly seems like people do want to change how Kolibri behaves - and it would be nice to prevent people from feeling the need to fork to achieve this) - it remains to be seen whether we can build a sufficiently tractable plugin API that people feel comfortable working with, but I think unless we have very strong reasons not to, I would rather maintain this goal than abandon it (especially as not doing this introduces us as a strong bottleneck on customized use of Kolibri).

In terms of possibility, yes, it is getting closer. Now that it is possible to build the frontend code without the Django stack, we are closer to that. Another important piece will be versioning and settling the coreAPI, and also then making that exportable so that it can be referenced by external plugins. I think you had some good ideas about limiting the scope of this (while still maintaining our somewhat catchall approach to the coreAPI that we currently have!)

From our discussion, I get the sense that perhaps the issues may be less about 'build time configuration' vs 'dynamic configuration', and maybe more about how much of this gets encoded in the frontend code vs the backend code? If the configuration of individual navigation items was done in frontend code would you still be as concerned about the use of build time configuration?

@kotfic thanks for the thoughtful explanation. From your book:

> The fundamental issue with the Traditional Method is how does one synchronize Celery's configuration across producer (Girder) and consumer (Girder Worker) processes, keeping in mind that these processes may be taking place on two (or more) different physical machines. Traditionally Celery solves this problem through the Python import mechanism.

This helps answer a question that has been nagging me: Requiring importing the task package has some clear downsides, but what are the upsides?

Do you envision supporting the "traditional approach" long term or deprecating it? I see the approaches as two sides of a coin. The traditional approach starts with a Girder Job and crams in all Celery Task information in a special, non-standard way. The new approach starts with a Celery Task and crams in all Girder Job information in a special, slightly non-standard way. The (good) argument is that Celery Tasks are more complex and nuanced than Girder Jobs, so we should normally be starting with the Celery Task.

I think some of the exposition you provide about how Girder and Girder Worker work together and what GW's function is should be put front and center in a revamp of GW docs.

This is because the container names for such components lack the @#.  So all n of router@# units start a container that's just named "router" and deisctl isn't smart enough to know that's going on.  The best solution, if docker allows it would be to make the container names always match the unit names... i.e. include the @#... but docker may not like special characters in container names.  Nevertheless, it's worth attempting before looking for a more compkex solution wherein deisctl needs specific logic for handling components with @# in their names.

Here you have two different Python installations and they are stepping on each other's toes.

I am guessing you elected to allow the installer to add itself to your system `PATH` but already had 'normal' Python 2.7 installed to the default location and also present in your `PATH`?

Unfortunately our installer adds its entries to the end of `PATH` so DLLs for other software frequently get loaded instead of ours (Python itself consists of many DLLs).

I think, if the Anaconda Installer is going to add to `PATH` it should add its entries to the front of `PATH` like nearly all other installers do, but I more strongly think that software should not add itself to `PATH` by default (or recommend that to users).

If you use the `Anaconda Prompt` to launch your command prompt then that will add the correct entries to the front of your `PATH` temporarily (i.e. for that command prompt instance only).

It also shows the true complexity of giving a good hint in every possible case. If you put a wrong value in a "hard" sudoku puzzle, the reason why it is wrong may be complicated, and it's quite common that removing any one of the known numbers makes it not wrong.

An issue we just noticed. OpenSSL versions (like 1.0.1k) are not 440 compliant, except, the ones that end in a, b, or c. Those ones, it treats as alpha versions. So when OpenSSL version 1.0.2a is released, conda will see it as less than version 1.0.2 (this is the case both for the current code and for PEP 440).

You are correct in your assessment that it is not well documented.  I shall have to explain this better.

Let me outline the process that happens now in 3.x. The flow is in [`index_selection.py`](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/cli/index_selection.py). Using the Click API, every argument/option is a decorator above the method.  Each of these decorators allows for a callback.  The callback for these options appends filters to an array (in the special context object: `ctx.obj['filters']`).  Once all of these filters are added, it then iterates through the list of filters against the working list of indices, sending the filter & current working list to the [`regex_iterate`](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/api/filter.py#L20-L68) method.  This iteratively filters the list of indices in one method, rather than in each method as in v2.

Of course, the index_selection module has a _lot_ of if statements because there are a ton of edge cases.  I started with a considerably cleaner method, but the edge cases made it uglier.  I'd still rather have it ugly and working, though.

```

    type: command
    short-summary: Update or clear SSL policy settings.
    parameters:
        - name: --cipher-suites
          populator-commands:

```

@yugangw-msft

The reason is that not every error that originates in a validator is a validation error (since validator itself is a misnomer) and thus printing the usage string is either completely inappropriate or misleading (when the usage string doesn't actually convey the usage). In the case of change #1307 this was the first case, where the error was such that printing a usage string made no sense.

If you want the usage string printed, you can raise a ValueError instead of a CLIError.

The ServiceBusService class accepts an `authentication` parameter which lets you customize the signing of requests.
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/azure/servicebus/servicebusservice.py#L71

If it's specified, it will call `sign_request` on that object.

If it's None, it will instantiate either the old `ServiceBusWrapTokenAuthentication` when `account_key` and `issuer` are given, or `ServiceBusSASAuthentication` for `shared_access_key_name` + ``shared_access_key_value` pair:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/azure/servicebus/servicebusservice.py#L1185

You could create your own authentication class, inspired or derived from `ServiceBusSASAuthentication`, which will give you more control over how the `Authorization` header is built.

Sorry we don't have all the fancy bells and whistles that the C# version has, but hopefully this points you in the right direction.

import json

data = json.loads(data)
raw_data = data['rawData']

res = {}
for key in ['fortune', 'plaintext', 'db', 'update', 'json', 'query']:
    results = []
    d = raw_data[key]
    for name, val in d.items():
        #if len(val) < 5:
        #    continue
        info = val[0]
        s, e = info['startTime'], info['endTime']
        if 'totalRequests' not in info:
            continue

        reqs = info['totalRequests']
        persec = reqs / (e-s)
        results.append((persec, name, reqs))

    results.sort()
    res[key] = [(name, r1, r2) for (r1, name, r2) in list(reversed(sorted(results)))]

print(json.dumps(res))
```

> Assuming that I'm adamant about MathML, how would I ensure that the required files are accessible to the frontend?

You would need to modify the notebook's build process to change what files are installed, then run `python setup.py install`. [This is where we whitelist MathJax files](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/4.0.0/setupbase.py#L150).

Since we do the slimming at install time, instead of the above, you will have all of MathJax available if you do a development install:

    git clone https://github.com/jupyter/notebook
    cd notebook
    python setup.py js css
    pip install -e .

which only links to the source dir, instead of copying the whitelisted files into site-packages.

> Why a stripped copy of MathJax?

Size. A complete MathJax install is many times larger than the entirety of the notebook and all other dependencies combined. A slimmed version without image fonts is still more than everything else combined. So we follow the recommended MathJax process for stripping the size of an install by only including the files/fonts we use at install time.

We could add MathML as an additional bundled output format, since it is not especially large.

Django uses `Python Database API Specification v2.0` in drivers. It prefers tuples for query rows returned by `fetch*` instead of dictionaries. See this answer in [DB API FAQ](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#frequently-asked-questions): case insensitive names in many databases and unexped names of database generated columns like aggregation functions. Django also uses tuples and sometimes adds also dictionary keys, sometimes use only indexes internally. We solved many features directly in ORM by reverse-engineering and it was easier than to implement any intermediate low level standard layer, BUT Django releases a new version every 8 months that requires to debug, rethink and refactor approximately one third of our code, because the ORM is substantionally improved regularly. The SQL and SOQL references are changing much slower. I see that the current Salesforce backend require more maintenance than all standard backends together.

A naive temporary fix is probably still possible with many restrictions, but I prefer to write a new stable layer that will imitate the basic principles of the DB API. The current SOQL compiler is the first and successful step. The soap and bulk api is importand not only due to speed but also to prevent governor limits for APIs, otherwise you can only click on SFDC web for that company for 24 hours without using any API. I want to be ready with this before [Django 1.10 -.Schedule: July 2016](https://code.djangoproject.com/wiki/Version1.10Roadmap#schedule). Versions 1.8 - 1.10 will be supported.

@contextmanager
def tee_output_fd():
    with NamedTemporaryFile() as target:
        original_stdout_fd = 1

        saved_stdout_fd = os.dup(original_stdout_fd)

        flush()
        os.dup2(target.fileno(), original_stdout_fd)
        out = CapturedStdout(target)

        try:

        finally:

            os.dup2(saved_stdout_fd, original_stdout_fd)

Some tableau formulations assume that all variables are non-negative. They introduce dual copies or compensation variables to solve also for negative solutions. So I guess you are solving a system where variables are assumed to be non-negative. 
When you declare a variable to be real in Z3 it ranges over all real numbers. You can, as you correctly did, assert that variables are non-negative by adding inequalities. 

To see that the problem is unbounded for arbitrary reals you can do the following:

solve for w in: w - y >= 10, which is w = 10 + y, so the rest of the system is:
x + y + z + 10 + y <= 40,
2x + y - z - 10 - y <= 10

Then solve for z in the last inequality: z = 2x - 20, and substitute the solution in the remaining:

x + 2y + 2x - 20 + 10 <= 40
which simplifies to

2x + 2y <= 60

Go back to your objective function:

    1/2)x + 3y + z + 4w
then you see that it rewrites to:

     1/2*x + 3y + 2x - 20 + 40+4y

So the coefficient to y is larger than the coefficient to x, thus, you can always set x = -y and make y as large as possible.

A lot of stuff happens between `sim_manager.run()` and `BackendZ3._batch_eval`. This can include (but isn't limited to):

1. The constraints might be split into independent constraint sets, which are iterated over and solved by sub-solvers with in no particular order (nondeterministic behavior, as rhelmot mentioned). This means that each sub-solver would overwrite the file, and the latest file to survive is more or less random.
2. The constraint solve might be handled by a cache, never reaching Z3 at all.
3. The solve might have arbitrary extra constraints added for whatever reason (especially during symbolic memory resolution and the checking of unsatisfiable states).

You can mitigate the first issue by removing `COMPOSITE_SOLVER` from state options (using the `remove_options` argument, or by also calling `state.solver.reload_solver()` after modifying `state.options`). This will at least keep the constraints together.

You can mitigate the second issue by adding `CACHELESS_SOLVER` to state options (using the `add_options` argument, or by also calling `state.solver.reload_solver()` after modifying `state.options`).

There's nothing that can be done with the third issue --- angr can do weird stuff with the constraints any time it wants, and as `batch_eval` is basically called for any solve it does, for whatever purposes, you can't expect the constraints to be exactly those constraints on your state.

If you want constraints that make sense for the states involved, I would recommend creating a `ConstraintDumper` exploration technique that dumps constraints at every step. You can use `backend_z3.convert` to convert the full set of constraints (`state.solver.constraints`) to Z3 and dump them to SMT, for each state at every step, independent of the issues above and whatever optimizations and weirdness angr and claripy do during the solving process.

Hi @tchapi,

Even though I'm not an expert, here is a small contribution.

I think your main problem is as you said : 
_"It looks kind of a reflection of IR [...]"_ 
If this "blind spot" only appears when you try to scan special/reflective surfaces such as your PMMA, then I don't think you need to look any further. I'm pretty sure that the IR is somehow reflected by those surfaces and the camera is "blinding itself".

It's actually really easy to check, as @dorodnic said : 
_"Could you please share the infrared images using the Viewer?"_
I would also suggest that you provide the RGB images, because you might simply see the reflection of the camera itself. If so, please check if the "blind spot" is actually superposed to the IR emitter location on the RGB image.
I might be wrong, but that's my best suggestion.

If the problem is not corrected, you could also try to adjust (if possible) this parameters : 
Exposure
Gain
Laser Power

I would also point out : 
_"obviously, glass is a no go since it's opaque to IR"_
I think this statement can be discussed because the SR300 laser wavelength is actually 860 nm, which is categorized as Near Infrared. The almighty Google says that this type of IR is not (or nearly not) affected by glass. Although, for greater wavelengths (such as those used for thermal imaging), you are right. 
You might then be OK to do further tests with glass :)
Can somebody else confirm that ?
Thanks. Would you happen to know if that method is exposed in C#? I've been searching for it but can't find it in the source or docs...

I'm sorry I can't provide a video, but you can easily reproduce these conditions yourself.

I simply put a cup in front of the object a want to scan (the box). The goal is to ignore anything too close to the camera (the cup), see by yourself : 

Here are some screenshots : 
"Situation 1" plastic cup in front of the box + no "Depth Clamp Min"
![situation 1 all sensors](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36759618/43184414-d2b855da-8fe8-11e8-9555-5732da1edb27.PNG)
![situation 1 texture](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36759618/43184416-d2d8c8ce-8fe8-11e8-98ba-f42a471ad084.PNG)
![situation 1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36759618/43184417-d2f8a0d6-8fe8-11e8-8320-10a7cba875ba.PNG)

"Situation 2" plastic cup in front of the box + "Depth Clamp Min" set to 3400 (340 mm) to ignore the cup
![situation 2 all sensors](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36759618/43184460-f756b990-8fe8-11e8-8853-7858c0c1398c.PNG)
![situation 2 texture](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36759618/43184461-f7821072-8fe8-11e8-9acf-d62f897ad9b1.PNG)

I found another problem here, when returning an derived instance of an unregistered type as an offset base class pointer to a registered type.  We would cast to most-derived, pass that to `cast(...)`, which would then recognize that the type is unregistered and so cast it to the backup type.  But that isn't valid: by the time we get to `cast()` there's no way to get back to the base class pointer.

The update moves the type decision logic out of cast entirely: we should dynamic cast if the type is polymorphic *and* a registered type; otherwise we don't dynamic_cast at all, keeping the `itype` pointer as-is, and use `itype` as the type.  (The updated commit also adds another a test that triggers this case).

I think the issue is mainly that the current implementation of `certbot-dns-rfc2136` starts searching for the proper `SOA` record from the domain up. It is currently skipping the `_acme-challenge` subdomain, and when it finds the most specific delegation apex, it will use this as a base, creating a `TXT` `ResourceRecordSet` with a name `_acme-challenge`. 

Unfortunately the current functionality does not work with the configuration described in the issue, as the most specific `SOA` record it's able to find because of the reasons described above, is `example.com` instead of `_acme-challenge.example.com`. If the delegation apex would be `first.example.com` for the domains `*.first.example.com` (hence the challenge records being `_acme-challenge.first.example.com` and so on, this would work.

You'll have the option of using `--nginx certonly`, which under the hood will get you certs by doing:

1. add new vhosts to your running nginx config to pass tls-sni challenges
2. soft reload conf
3. passing authentication / obtain certs
4. revert nginx conf to exact prior state
5. soft reload conf

Or you'll be able to run without `certonly` in which case the following extra steps will occur:

6. modify config to use new certs
7. soft reload, check for detectable nginx conf errors
8. if errors are detected, revert config

I could finally reproduce this with "tagtool.py -v" (full memory print) and rapid removal of the tag while it's being read out. The fundamental problem is of course that the tag is no longer in communication range, the difference in nfcpy behaviour will be that a TagCommandError(TIMOUT_ERROR) exception is than raised.

Note that I was only able to verify with full memory print done by tag.dump() and that it only could happen for the tag header data reads because there's a loop that doesn't break on error (in the first place a read() call returned RECEIVE_ERROR and that is ignored).  Code that stops on tag communication errors (like the NDEF read logic in nfcpy) shouldn't have this problem. You've noticed that the design of the `.travis.yml` is fundamentally limited. Tox's configuration is far more expressive. However, I don't see a way to really make it better. I do typically take the approach of _including_ extra jobs instead of _excluding_ jobs for deprecated variants, but which is shorter will depend on exactly what needs to be run.

The limitations that I've most run into in designing a good interface stem from the limitations of the Travis config file mostly. It really is frustrating, but I hesitate to try and come up with a new format, instead of using and understanding the native config files.

----

I don't in principle have an issue with coming up with some conventions to simplify the configuration.  However, I find myself hesitant in the case for a couple of reasons. First, who am I to decide which convention is right for a project to use with Tox? In your example, you have used `djangoXX` for the env names, whereas I have preferred to use `djXX`. In the case of the Python variants, it's Tox that has chosen, not me, and I just don't feel qualified to choose that for the community.

Second, when that happens the question becomes: which frameworks need to have this shortcut? Either the list is very selective, or it ends up becoming very long. I'm not sure I see a great rule on how to handle that.

---

As far as generating the env and matrix sections of the Travis yml, We'd really have to know about _all_ the factors that Travis uses to generate a matrix, so that really would be a wide-scoped issue.  I totally agree that Travis' configuration for excludes and environments is unfortunately verbose, but I don't see a great way around it.

Generating the Travis config is probably the best way around it, but it's sufficiently messy of a proposition that I haven't been inclined to undertake it, especially because the _generated_ file would still need to be checked in the repository (after being verified). I don't know that it would really decrease the burden on the user. However, I'm not opposed to it entirely, so if there was someone that really wanted to see a generator in Tox-Travis, I would consider, and likely accept, a pull request to add it.

Hi there,

Thanks for the interest and report. It sounds like a useful feature and have also been thinking of ways to improve the current parallel copy filename functionality.

First, let's see how this can be done currently, with a bit more code to write:

```
from gevent import spawn, joinall

client = ParallelSSHClient(<..>)

host_paths = {
    'host1': {'remote_file': 'a_file',
              'local_file':  'host1/a_file',
              'recurse': True},
    'host2': {'remote_file': 'b_file', 'local_file': 'host2_b_file'},
    'host3': {'local_file':  '/another/dir/host3_file'},
}

copies = [spawn(client.host_clients[host].copy_remote_file, 
          host_paths[host]['remote_file'], 
          host_paths[host]['local_file'], 
          recurse=host_paths[host].get('recurse', False})
    for host in host_paths]
joinall(copies, raise_error=True)
```

`client.host_clients[host]` is the `SSHClient` for that host giving direct access to its `copy_remote_file` function and allows for both filenames to be specified. 

This pattern can be used to extend parallel client with new functions, overloading existing ones and so on.

That should give a workaround while direct support is implemented.

As for implementation, here is what I had in mind for this functionality:

* Allow a string pattern to be specified for either or both local and remote file with formatting arguments to be used, like `host_args` command substitution functionality. This can include sub directories. Similar to the above proposal but with formatting for string substitution rather than overrides.

Example use:

```
copy_args = [
    {'remote_file': 'file_a', 'local_file': 'a_sub_dir/host1_file'}
    {'remote_file': 'file_b', 'local_file': 'file_b'}
]

client.copy_remote_file('%(remote_file)s', '%(local_file)s', 
    recurse=True, copy_args=copy_args)
```

Where `client.hosts[0]` would get remote and local file format arguments from `copy_args[0]` and so on. Multiple formatting arguments can also be used. Must have same number of `copy_args` as number of hosts.

How does this look for the use case? Would prefer a substitution method rather than optionally overriding as it is more explicit with what is supposed to happen when there is a string substitution format in the file arguments.

Otherwise, a new function only taking per-host arguments would be best IMO.

I debugged this for a bit, and I think the problem may lie in the handling for storing the callbacks associated with the display ids:

https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/ee419c0a1714b45865a314bca3f941b6bf4485cf/notebook/static/services/kernels/kernel.js#L1171

https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/ee419c0a1714b45865a314bca3f941b6bf4485cf/notebook/static/services/kernels/kernel.js#L1150

In order for output to be displayed in response to a widget action, we have a hack that essentially appends the output to the cell containing the widget view that initiated the action. However, that means that any new updateable displays need to register the parent message id of the *containing* cell, not the widget callback (essentially, our hack is changing the output area containing the widget, without some of this bookkeeping for adding output to a cell). If the cell has another updateable display with the same id displayed independent of the widget, then things work fine because that cell's output area is registered as a place to look for display updates for that id (i.e., if you change your cell displaying the widget to:

```python
# In[6]:
display('updateable display', 'some_destination')
display(w)
```
then everything work likes you expect).


I checked, and display updates work just fine with output widgets - so there's another work around that combines both widgets and updateable displays.

Semaphores do have practical usage patterns that don't fall into the Sofa pattern -- for example, trio.Queue uses a Semaphore whose value starts at zero and then `put` releases and `get` acquires, so it tracks how many items are available in the Queue's buffer.

But maybe it would make sense to have Sofa be a more specialized and friendly Semaphore, that enforces that a task can't release unless it has already acquired? (`trio.Lock` already departs from `threading.Lock` in this regard... a `threading.Lock` can be released by any thread, even one that didn't acquire it. `trio.Lock` is pickier.)

FWIW, there's a trending topic on reddit/python: [What are some WTFs (still) in Python 3?](https://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/5zk97l/what_are_some_wtfs_still_in_python_3/)

Top things people seem to struggle with:

1. `is` vs `==`. Why `1 is 1` isn't the same as `1000 is 1000`.
2. Arguments defaults are calculated once: `def a(foo=[])`.
3. Bindings in for loops: `[(lambda: i) for i in range(10)]` (this one bugs even advanced users).
4. Operators chaining: `"a" in "aa" in "aaa"`.

I believe most of those issues (including forgetting to put `await` or parens) should be handled by IDEs and tools like mypy.

Have a look here https://github.com/Scille/parsec-cloud/blob/54c34a3cdf69e4735e681a4ecf6c767eef57a0df/parsec/core/fs/folder.py#L193
I'm building a fuse-based filesystem with cloud synchronization. The idea is to have a local version of your files (that can be only a part of your entire files tree and can be access/modified offline) and a remote one.

I use rw-lock here to lock a single file/folder given working on it can mean accessing to remote data.
For instance reading a file means we must fetch the metadata (that may or may not be available locally) then use those info to fetch the data that are splitted in multiple chunks.
In the meantime a synchronization process can occur which aim at uploading a new version of file metadata and data chunks.

> In many cases atomicity of writes (e.g. on some complex data structure) can be enforced by not having any checkpoint within the write operation

Since the post of this issue, I've rewritten my code following this idea to get rid of the rwlocks. The idea was to make copy of data before doing any await and rely on merge algorithm (see https://github.com/Scille/parsec-cloud/blob/8ec6d260852971e8379d230f4614a3b5f2b08bc6/parsec/core/fs2/__init__.py#L449).

One idea for nurseries-in-generators is to provide an ergonomic way to get a context-managed generator. I wrote an example implementation up at https://gist.github.com/oremanj/772241665edea3ceb3183a4924aecc08 (it was originally intended to be a quick-and-dirty demo, but rather grew a life of its own...) I think something like this would likely be useful in a trio utilities library. It doesn't require any special support from trio or async_generator and is rather large/magical so I'm not sure it deserves to make it into trio itself, unless trio wants to use it.

There are still notable limitations; for example, in the azip() example, if one of the inner slow_counters reaches its deadline and is cancelled, the whole azip() will be cancelled, because we had to enter the slow_counter contexts to get async iterables that could be passed to the azip() context. But if the limitations are acceptable, cancel scopes and nurseries that are attached to a generator in this fashion should be guaranteed to nest correctly / not mess up the cancel stack.

Just pushed a fix. How I tracked it down:

I looked at the two failing tests. The one testing `start` is super complicated (and `start` itself is ... kind of a mess, honestly), but the `test_serve_tcp` is simpler so I focused on it. If you look at the traceback in the pytest logs, it's this weird `Cancelled` exception that shouldn't be escaping – it should be getting caught by the `NurseryManager`. And while this test does involve a background task and `start`... the traceback for the stray exception shows it starting out in the main task. The things that show up are:

```python
async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
    async with stream:
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
    # The dedent here exits the 'async with stream' block, which calls 'await stream.aclose()'
    # This call raises 'Cancelled' (as it should, since we just cancelled the surrounding scope).
    # And then this exception escapes the nursery block, which is the bug.
```

So I tried writing a minimal version of this:

```python
import trio

async def main():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
        await trio.sleep(0)

trio.run(main)
```

and indeed, this crashes the same way. So now we have a minimal reproducer.

I still had absolutely no idea what was going on though. How come this Cancelled exception wasn't being caught by the cancel scope? So next I stuck a pdb breakpoint at the top of `NurseryManager.__aexit__`, and started single-stepping. I checked that the exception was actually being caught, that `exc._scope` matched `nursery.cancel_scope`, so it should be caught correctly, I eventually stepped into the `MultiError.catch` call inside `open_cancel_scope`... and found that it actually *did* catch the exception correctly. So... somehow the exception is later being magically resurrected out of nowhere?

So I squinted hard at `NurseryManager.__aexit__` again, trying to figure out which path it would take: if `self._scope_manager.__exit__` actually is catching the exception... then it should be returning `True`, to suppress it... and so the `if not` should *not* be taken so... then we fall through and return `None`, which tells Python to let the original exception keep propagating, which in this case... oh yeah, that's our resurrected exception, duh.

> `put_nowait` is very useful when called from an asyncio callback.

Do you mean the queue that `trio-asyncio` uses to send requests back to the main asyncio loop dispatcher? I'm not 100% sure if that's in scope or not, since it really should be using an unbounded queue... but then I've also been wondering if we should go ahead and allow for unbounded channels, e.g. by explicitly passing `capacity=inf`. (The example that's got me thinking this: consider a web crawler that keeps a queue of URLs to crawl. Each worker takes a URL off the queue, fetches it, parses the page, and pushes the resulting URLs back onto the queue to get to later. We put a limit on how many workers run at once. That's enough to produce deadlocks with any finite queue capacity, if the queue fills up so no workers can push new URLs onto it, so they stop pulling items off the queue...) So fair enough.

>  get_nowait also has its use; during connection shutdown one might want to drain the queue and log the pending messages without introducing cancel points.

Yeah, mayyybe... this kind of consideration is why I'm more likely to keep `get_nowait` than `full` or `task_done` or whatever. But I'm not convinced yet that what you said is something that really happens – like if you're doing a graceful shutdown, you want to signal that to the producer and then let it finish draining the queue until it gets `EndOfQueue`. "All the items that are currently in the queue" is not really a reliable concept because of race conditions. And if you've decided to cancel everything and abandon the queue without waiting for `EndOfQueue`, then there's not much point in worrying about this.

I guess `get_nowait` is potentially useful for `trio-asyncio` too, if you want to make sure you do one tick of the asyncio loop per tick of the trio loop...

@jeromekelleher I think that's a variation on the first option. I.e., the first option says, numcodecs defines two codec formats for JSON, a legacy format registered under the "json" ID, and a new format registered under a new ID (e.g., "json2"). Under this option, numcodecs also provides two codec classes, one which implements the legacy "json" format, another which implements the new "json2" format. The next question is about API compatibility, i.e., what should these codec classes be called. Your suggestion is to have a class called ``Json`` implementing the new format, and keeping the old class called ``JSON`` implementing the old format. There are other choices we could make here, e.g., have a class called ``JSON`` implement the new format, and a class called something like ``LegacyJSON`` implementing the old format (and probably hidden in the API docs). These would have different consequences regarding how old code would run following a numcodecs upgrade. Not sure what is best yet, just trying to understand the options and consequences.

I think this is pretty easy to fix - what seems to be happening is that there is a DEBUG log message in the nmslib.cc indexwrapper destructor (https://github.com/nmslib/nmslib/blob/master/python_bindings/nmslib.cc#L351). This attempts to interface with the python logging infrastructure, but the destructor is being called because the process is being cleaned up (it's in PyImport_Cleanup), and in python 3.6.5 this causes an exception deep in pybind11.

Removing that debug statement remove the error, but isn't a satisfactory fix. I don't have any more time today, but will get a proper fix in later this week.
